title: Moss Helps Catch Grave Robbers in a Cemetery
summary: At Burr Oak Cemetery in Chicago, some workers were caught digging up old graves to sell the spaces again, which is called grave robbing. This happened in 2009, and they were caught in 2015. Scientists used moss, a small green plant, to help prove when the graves were disturbed. Moss can tell time because it changes as it gets older, even when buried. The FBI asked a museum expert to study the moss found with the bones. This helped show when the robbers moved the remains. The cemetery is special because famous people like Emmett Till are buried there. This story shows how tiny plants can help solve big crimes.

background_read: Grave robbing is a crime where people dig up graves, often to steal valuables or resell burial plots. Mosses are small, non-flowering plants that can survive in many places and hold clues about their environment. Forensic science uses evidence like plants to solve crimes by studying details like age and location. Cemeteries are sacred places for burying the dead, and disturbing them is illegal and disrespectful. The FBI is a federal agency in the U.S. that investigates serious crimes. This case shows how science can help justice by using nature's clues.
Article_Structure: WHO: Workers at Burr Oak Cemetery and scientists like Matt von Konrat
WHAT: Grave robbing where old graves were dug up and moss helped prove it
WHERE: Burr Oak Cemetery in Chicago, Illinois
WHY: To catch criminals and show how moss can be used in forensic science

perspectives.description: Moss provides evidence to solve crimes by showing time and habitat details.
